confirmed.detail: Lines 145-172 extend `NowPlayingPlaybackShellController` (bindCleanSongTitlePreference) inside the file named for NowPlayingShellController, which already hosts four other declarations (NowPlayingShellController, NowPlayingQueueActionPresenting plus two conformance extensions, NowPlayingQueueShellController with its default implementation). The repo's convention is filename = export with Type+Feature.swift extensions; a maintainer looking for playback-shell behavior in NowPlayingPlaybackShellController.swift will not find this binding.
confirmed.suggestion: Move the bindCleanSongTitlePreference extension into NowPlayingPlaybackShellController.swift, and consider splitting the NowPlayingQueueActionPresenting protocol + conformances into their own file.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Verified: NowPlayingShellController.swift:145-172 extends NowPlayingPlaybackShellController with bindCleanSongTitlePreference, while the dedicated NowPlayingPlaybackShellController.swift in the same Support directory hosts the protocol and ALL of its sibling bind* methods (bindContentSelector, bindQueueSnapshot, bindPlaybackSnapshot, bindPlaybackTime) in an identically-constrained extension. Grep confirms these are the only two extension sites for the protocol. Every other Support file (Artwork/Lyric/Lifecycle/Transport shell controllers) only extends protocols declared in the same file, so this is the lone cross-file stray and a maintainer scanning NowPlayingPlaybackShellController.swift for bindings will miss it. The primary fix (move the extension into NowPlayingPlaybackShellController.swift) matches both AGENTS.md's responsibility-split rule and the module's dominant convention. Caveat: the secondary suggestion to split NowPlayingQueueActionPresenting + conformances into their own file should be dropped — bundling a feature's Presenting protocol with its shell protocol in one file is the deliberate local convention (see NowPlayingArtworkShellController.swift hosting NowPlayingArtworkShellPresenting). Severity stays low: discoverability nit only, no behavioral or misleading-logic impact.

confirmed.title: updateInterfaceSuspensionState takes an inout parameter shadowing the protocol property of the same name
confirmed.detail: `func updateInterfaceSuspensionState(_ suspended: Bool, isInterfaceSuspended: inout Bool)` exists only because NowPlayingPlaybackShellController declares `var isInterfaceSuspended: Bool { get }` (read-only), so the mixin cannot write it. Each conformer therefore defines setInterfaceSuspended that passes `&isInterfaceSuspended` back into the helper (NowPlayingCompactController.swift:154, NowPlayingRelaxedController.swift:226). The inout parameter named identically to the protocol property makes the data flow needlessly hard to follow.
confirmed.suggestion: Change the protocol requirement to `var isInterfaceSuspended: Bool { get set }` (conformers already store it), let the default implementation assign it directly, and delete the inout parameter plus the per-controller setInterfaceSuspended forwarding.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Verified: the inout helper, the read-only protocol requirement, and the per-controller setInterfaceSuspended forwarders all exist as cited. The shadowing is real, and the same protocol already uses { get set } for lastPresentedTrackID/lastPresentedArtworkURL with direct assignment from the protocol extension, so the suggested fix aligns with (not against) the module's dominant convention. The private(set) encapsulation counter-argument fails because sibling mixin-mutated properties are already plain var. AGENTS.md does not endorse the inout pattern. Additionally, inout copy-in/copy-out means callees inside the helper would observe a stale self.isInterfaceSuspended — a latent trap, though no current callee reads it, so severity remains low.

confirmed.title: Empty lifecycle overrides and empty deinit are dead code
confirmed.detail: viewDidAppear (lines 104-106) and viewDidLayoutSubviews (lines 108-110) only call super, and `deinit {}` (line 127) is empty. The same pattern appears in NowPlayingRelaxedController.swift (viewDidLayoutSubviews 108-110, deinit 139), LyricTimelineView.swift layoutSubviews (183-185), and EdgeFadeBlurView.swift layoutSubviews (28-30). Each one signals customization that does not exist.
confirmed.suggestion: Delete the no-op overrides and empty deinits in all four files.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Verified all cited locations: NowPlayingCompactController.swift has viewDidAppear (104-106) and viewDidLayoutSubviews (108-110) that only call super plus an empty deinit (127); NowPlayingRelaxedController.swift mirrors this (108-110, 139); LyricTimelineView.swift (actually at Interface/NowPlaying/LyricTimeline/, lines 183-185) and EdgeFadeBlurView.swift (28-30) have layoutSubviews overrides that only call super. Refutation failed on all fronts: (1) repo convention is the opposite — every other viewDidAppear/viewDidLayoutSubviews/layoutSubviews override in the codebase (~15 occurrences) does real work, so these no-ops are anomalies that falsely signal customization; (2) AGENTS.md contains no endorsement of placeholder overrides or empty deinits; (3) removal is behaviorally identical (super-only overrides and empty deinit are semantically equivalent to absence; no protocol requires them). EdgeFadeBlurView's traitCollectionDidChange is a deliberate super-skip with a comment, but that is not part of the finding. Severity remains low: cosmetic clarity issue, does not actively mislead about runtime behavior.

confirmed.title: No-op weak-self wrapper around onToggleShuffle diverges from the relaxed controller
confirmed.detail: installQueueActionHandlers wraps only onToggleShuffle in `{ [weak self] in guard self != nil else { return }; onToggleShuffle() }` (lines 140-143) while forwarding the other six handlers directly — and NowPlayingRelaxedController.swift:150-159 forwards all seven directly. The wrapper adds no behavior (the closures built in bindQueueSectionActions already capture self weakly) and makes a reader hunt for a difference that does not exist.
confirmed.suggestion: Pass onToggleShuffle straight through like the other handlers, matching the relaxed controller.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: NowPlayingCompactController.installQueueActionHandlers wraps only onToggleShuffle in a [weak self] guard-nil wrapper while passing the other six handlers directly, whereas NowPlayingRelaxedController forwards all seven directly. The wrapper is provably a no-op: the only caller, bindQueueSectionActions in NowPlayingShellController.swift:91, already builds onToggleShuffle as { [weak self] in self?.shuffleQueueOnce() } where self is the same controller instance, so the closure already does nothing after dealloc. The asymmetry misleads readers into searching for a nonexistent behavioral difference. No AGENTS.md rule or local convention endorses the wrapper; the dominant convention (relaxed controller plus the six sibling handlers) is direct pass-through, so the suggested fix aligns with repo style. Cosmetic only, no functional risk.

confirmed.title: Trailing guards with empty bodies are leftover dead code in three methods
confirmed.detail: applySupplementalPlaybackProgress ends with `guard controlIslandViewModel.selectedContentSelector == .lyrics else { return }` followed by nothing (lines 28-29); refreshPlayingContent ends with `guard selector == .lyrics else { return }` followed by nothing (lines 88-90); and NowPlayingRelaxedController+Playback.swift:29-31 has an entire body that is just `guard currentRightPanel == .lyrics else { return }`. These are remnants of removed lyric-progress pushes and mislead readers into believing lyric-specific work happens behind them.
confirmed.suggestion: Delete the trailing guards; make the relaxed applySupplementalPlaybackProgress an explicit empty implementation (or remove the requirement for controllers that need no supplemental progress).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: All three cited locations verified verbatim: NowPlayingCompactController+Playback.swift line 28 (trailing guard ending applySupplementalPlaybackProgress) and lines 88-90 (trailing guard ending refreshPlayingContent), plus NowPlayingRelaxedController+Playback.swift lines 29-31 where the entire body is one guard. Each is a guard...else { return } followed by no statements — a functional no-op that implies lyric-specific work which does not exist. applySupplementalPlaybackProgress is a protocol requirement (NowPlayingPlaybackShellController.swift:49) invoked on every playback snapshot/progress update (lines 65, 144, 181), so readers tracing lyric progress flow will land here and be misled. Project instructions endorse guards for reducing nesting, not dead guards; no local convention endorses this. The suggested fix (delete the guards, keep an explicitly empty relaxed implementation since the protocol requires it) is behavior-preserving and consistent with repo style. Medium severity stands: it actively misleads maintainers about where lyric progress is handled.

confirmed.title: makeShowLyricsAction/makeShowPlaybackQueueAction duplicated verbatim across both controllers
confirmed.detail: Lines 93-109 are byte-identical to NowPlayingRelaxedController+Playback.swift:75-91 (same titles, SF symbols, and controlIslandViewModel.setContentSelector bodies), and the surrounding updateTransportSongMenu construction is also largely repeated. Any wording or symbol change must now be made twice.
confirmed.suggestion: Hoist both factories (and the shared songContextMenuProvider menu assembly) into an extension on NowPlayingPlaybackShellController where Self: UIViewController, since both controllers already conform and own controlIslandViewModel.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: The duplication is verified byte-for-byte: makeShowLyricsAction/makeShowPlaybackQueueAction in NowPlayingCompactController+Playback.swift:93-109 are identical to NowPlayingRelaxedController+Playback.swift:75-91 (same localized titles, SF symbols, setContentSelector bodies). The suggested hoist target already exists and is the module's established convention — NowPlayingPlaybackShellController.swift:55 has an `extension NowPlayingPlaybackShellController where Self: UIViewController` block holding shared logic for both controllers, controlIslandViewModel is a base NowPlayingShellController requirement, and both controllers conform. So the fix would make the code read MORE like the surrounding repo, not less. AGENTS.md does not endorse the duplication. However, severity is overstated: only the two small factories are pure duplication; the surrounding updateTransportSongMenu assemblies differ meaningfully (Compact adds a favorite/like section and targets pageViewController vs relaxedTransportView), and no drift has occurred. Adjusted to low (cosmetic maintenance, two adjacent files, trivial to sync).

confirmed.title: Stray empty MARK sections
confirmed.detail: Back-to-back `// MARK: - Right Panel` and `// MARK: - Layout Containers` (lines 65-67) precede a single property, and `// MARK: - Background Setup` (line 162) is immediately followed by `// MARK: - Content Layout` with no members between them — leftover section headers from moved code that now mislabel the file's structure in the Xcode jump bar.
confirmed.suggestion: Delete the empty MARK headers and keep only those that label actual sections.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Verified in NowPlayingRelaxedController.swift: lines 65-67 have back-to-back `// MARK: - Right Panel` / `// MARK: - Layout Containers` preceding a single property, and lines 162-164 have `// MARK: - Background Setup` immediately followed by `// MARK: - Content Layout` with no members between (installBackgroundView is defined in a protocol extension, not this file). AGENTS.md does not endorse empty MARK headers; the file's own dominant convention is MARKs labeling real sections, so removing the empty/stale headers aligns with local style. Cosmetic jump-bar mislabeling only.

confirmed.title: Trailing guard exists only to gate a misleading 'snapshot start' log
confirmed.detail: In updateQueue, `guard update.appliedSnapshot else { return }` (lines 76-78) is followed solely by an AppLog.info reading "queue snapshot start ..." (lines 80-83) — but updateQueuePresentation already applied the snapshot synchronously on line 66, so the "start" message fires after the work completed and the guard protects nothing else. Readers of the logs and of the code both get the wrong picture of ordering.
confirmed.suggestion: Fold the information into the existing "queue refresh apply" log (which already records identity/content/player change flags) and delete the trailing guard, or move the start log before updateQueuePresentation.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Verified in NowPlayingListSectionView.swift lines 56-84: updateQueuePresentation (line 66) applies the table snapshot synchronously (applyQueueSnapshot runs inline in NowPlayingQueueSectionView lines 264-269), yet the trailing guard at lines 76-78 gates only a "queue snapshot start" AppLog.info (lines 80-83) with nothing after it. The paired "queue snapshot finished" log fires inside didApplyQueueSnapshot, which runs synchronously for the first-load and content-only paths — so logs can show "finished" before "start", and even in the batch path the reload work has already executed when "start" prints. The guard-then-log-then-end structure misrepresents both control flow and log ordering. AGENTS.md's Logging Rules do not endorse this pattern, and surrounding code (the accurate "queue refresh apply" log at lines 71-74) follows correct ordering, so the fix would match repo conventions. Impact is diagnostics-only; severity low.

confirmed.title: +DataSource filename mislabels a snapshot-mapping helper
confirmed.detail: In this codebase a +DataSource suffix means UITableView/UICollectionView data-source conformance (the table data source for this view actually lives in superclass NowPlayingQueueSectionView). This file contains only makeQueueSnapshot — a PlaybackTrack-to-AMNowPlayingQueueSnapshot mapping wrapper — so the responsibility-based filename points readers to the wrong place.
confirmed.suggestion: Rename the file to NowPlayingListSectionView+Snapshot.swift (or fold the 15-line helper back into NowPlayingListSectionView.swift).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: NowPlayingListSectionView+DataSource.swift contains only makeQueueSnapshot, a PlaybackTrack-to-AMNowPlayingQueueSnapshot mapping wrapper used once (NowPlayingListSectionView.swift:61). The actual UITableViewDataSource conformance lives in superclass NowPlayingQueueSectionView (conformance at line 23, dataSource assignment at 157, MARK at 651). The finding slightly overstates by claiming an in-repo "+DataSource" convention — this is the only +DataSource file in the codebase — but AGENTS.md explicitly mandates responsibility-based extension naming (+Layout, +Actions, +Table, etc.), and in UIKit "+DataSource" universally connotes table/collection data-source methods, so the name does misdirect readers toward the wrong responsibility. The suggested rename to +Snapshot.swift (or inlining the 15-line single-use helper) conforms to AGENTS.md conventions and improves clarity. Impact is minor: the file is 27 lines, so the misdirection costs seconds, not bugs.

confirmed.title: Dead stored properties: displayedArtworkURL and an unused cancellables set
confirmed.detail: `private var displayedArtworkURL: URL?` (line 8) is never read or written anywhere, and the outer class's `private var cancellables = Set<AnyCancellable>()` (line 9) is never stored into — the only Combine subscription lives in the nested NowPlayingArtworkImageView, which has its own set (line 41). Both properties imply state tracking that does not exist.
confirmed.suggestion: Delete both properties (and the then-unneeded Combine import usage on the outer type).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Verified in /Users/qaq/Documents/GitHub/MuseAmp/MuseAmp/Interface/NowPlaying/Sections/NowPlayingAvatarSectionView.swift: `displayedArtworkURL` (line 8) appears only at its declaration repo-wide (grep confirms no reads/writes), and the outer class's `cancellables` (line 9) is private and never stored into — the sole `.store(in: &cancellables)` at line 52 belongs to the nested private NowPlayingArtworkImageView with its own set at line 41. AGENTS.md does not endorse dead state; its Property Rules section ("Avoid unnecessary optionals", "Do not introduce stored properties to track state that is already available") actually supports removal. The per-class cancellables rule applies only when subscriptions exist. Minor correction to the suggestion: `import Combine` cannot be removed since the nested class in the same file uses it, but deleting the two dead properties is correct and clarifying. Severity stays low — cosmetic dead state that mildly implies tracking that doesn't exist, no bug risk.

confirmed.title: Identical button-configuration boilerplate repeated across all four control buttons
confirmed.detail: PlayPausePlayerControlButton (lines 7-17), NextPlayerControlButton, PreviousPlayerControlButton, and FavoritePlayerControlButton each repeat the same 10-line block: plain configuration, white baseForegroundColor, zero contentInsets, symbol configuration differing only in pointSize, white tintColor, setImage, accessibilityLabel. Three of them also repeat the `alpha = isEnabled ? 1 : 0.1` disabled treatment in bindState — and 0.1 already has a name (NowPlayingTransportView.Layout.unavailableTransportButtonAlpha) that none of them use.
confirmed.suggestion: Add a `configureSymbolAppearance(systemName:pointSize:accessibilityLabel:)` helper and a `setAvailable(_:)` helper (using Layout.unavailableTransportButtonAlpha) on the PlayerControlButton base class, and call them from the subclasses.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Verified: all four PlayerControlButton subclasses repeat the identical ~10-line configuration block (plain config, white baseForegroundColor, zero contentInsets, symbol config differing only in pointSize 30/20/20/16, white tintColor, setImage, accessibilityLabel). Three subclasses (Next, Previous, Favorite) hardcode `alpha = isEnabled ? 1 : 0.1` in bindState while NowPlayingTransportView.Layout.unavailableTransportButtonAlpha (= 0.1) exists and is already used by the sibling NowPlayingRoutePickerContainerView for the same disabled treatment — a genuine inconsistency and drift hazard. AGENTS.md does not endorse the duplication; the suggested helpers on the existing PlayerControlButton base class fit the repo's existing pattern (the base class already centralizes tap wiring, haptics, and cancellables), so the fix would not read against local convention. Impact is cosmetic clarity, so severity stays low.

confirmed.title: Tag-based button dispatch with magic 1/2 instead of identity comparison
confirmed.detail: Buttons are assigned `button.tag = tag + 1` in a loop (line 80) and handleTap switches on `sender.tag` cases 1 and 2 (lines 159-166) to choose between onShuffleTap and onRepeatTap. The numeric indirection forces readers to reconstruct loop order to know which tag is which, when the two buttons are already stored properties.
confirmed.suggestion: Drop the tags and dispatch on identity (`if sender === shuffleButton { onShuffleTap() } else if sender === repeatButton { onRepeatTap() }`), or use per-button UIAction handlers.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: NowPlayingQueueHeaderCell.swift lines 79-85 assign button.tag = tag + 1 in an enumerated loop and handleTap switches on sender.tag cases 1/2 (lines 159-166) to route to onShuffleTap/onRepeatTap, with a dead default branch — even though shuffleButton and repeatButton are stored properties. A repo-wide grep shows this is the ONLY tag-based dispatch in the codebase; sibling NowPlaying components (NowPlayingTransportView, NowPlayingCompactTransportView, PlayerControlButton) all use dedicated per-button selectors. AGENTS.md does not endorse tag dispatch and its style guidance (intention-revealing code, named constants over magic numbers) leans against it. The suggested fix (identity comparison or per-button handlers) would match the module's dominant convention. Impact is purely cosmetic in a two-button cell, so severity stays low.

refuted.title: Bootstrap creates parallel APIClient and EmbeddedMetadataReader instances that diverge from AppEnvironment's
refuted.reason: The duplication is factually present (Bootstrap builds apiClient/metadataReader at AppEnvironment+Bootstrap.swift:20-21; AppEnvironment.init builds a second pair at AppEnvironment.swift:59,62), but the finding's risk claim is refuted by the implementation: APIClient already has mutable auth/base-URL state and deliberately re-resolves it from AppPreferences.currentSubsonicConfiguration on every request via synchronizedState()/refreshConfigurationIfNeededLocked() (APIClient.swift:201-237), so multiple instances converge by construction — base-URL switching and auth are exactly the cases already handled. EmbeddedMetadataReader is fully stateless (no mutable properties). The pattern is also the deliberate dominant convention: the tvOS shell duplicates the same bootstrap factory (TVAppContext+Bootstrap.swift), and the boot flow intentionally constructs DatabaseManager before any environment exists (BootProgressController.swift:64 then AppEnvironment(databaseManager:)). The suggested tuple-returning init would complicate the boot seam in both targets to prevent a divergence that cannot occur, making the code read unlike the rest of the repo.

refuted.title: Async initializeDatabaseManager wrapper relies on invisible executor hopping
refuted.reason: The finding's factual core is wrong for this codebase. The app target builds with SWIFT_DEFAULT_ACTOR_ISOLATION = MainActor and SWIFT_APPROACHABLE_CONCURRENCY = YES (MuseAmp.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj). AppEnvironment is a plain final class with no nonisolated annotations in AppEnvironment+Bootstrap.swift, so both initializeDatabaseManagerSynchronously and the async initializeDatabaseManager are MainActor-isolated — the async wrapper does NOT hop off the caller's actor; the WCDB init already runs on the main actor during boot. Approachable Concurrency also already enables NonisolatedNonsendingByDefault, so the finding's hypothetical future hazard is the current, intentional behavior. AGENTS.md line 50 explicitly documents BootProgressController as a 'blocking library boot controller', confirming blocking-on-boot is the documented design (the tvOS counterpart even calls the sync variant directly). The suggested fix would either insert a false comment (claiming the function moves work off the caller's actor when it does not) or change behavior with Task.detached against the documented design. At most the wrapper is a mildly redundant async overload — cosmetic, not the described medium-severity executor hazard.

refuted.title: defaultAPIBaseURL is a sentinel placeholder, not a default
refuted.reason: The cited code exists (/Users/qaq/Documents/GitHub/MuseAmp/MuseAmp/Application/AppPreferences.swift:29) and example.com is indeed an inert RFC-2606 host. But the finding mischaracterizes the symbol's role. `defaultAPIBaseURL` is precisely the default argument value for the injectable `apiBaseURL` parameter — that is what "default" means here, and the name is semantically accurate. Tests (MuseAmpTests/AppPreferencesTests.swift:42-60) inject other URLs (e.g. fallback.example.com) into `APIClient(baseURL:)` and assert the client falls back to whatever was injected when no server is configured, confirming the value is a genuine injectable default, not a hardcoded sentinel concept that deserves a sentinel name at every call site. The inertness of the production default is a deliberate product fact (a Subsonic client has no shippable default server; the adjacent fallback credentials are empty strings, making non-functionality obvious), and the definition is one jump away and self-explanatory. Crucially, the rename would break the dominant deliberate naming convention of the whole configuration cluster: `defaultSubsonicServerURL` (AppPreferences.swift:31), `effectiveAPIBaseURL(fallback:)` (line 105), `APIClient.defaultBaseURL` and `resolveConfiguration(defaultBaseURL:)` (Backend/API/APIClient.swift:21,230), plus default-argument usage across AppEnvironment.swift, AppEnvironment+Bootstrap.swift, and MuseAmpTV/Application/TVAppContext*.swift. Renaming only `defaultAPIBaseURL` to `placeholderAPIBaseURL` (as suggested) would make one node of the cluster read differently from the rest ("placeholder" feeding a parameter called "defaultBaseURL"/"fallback"), producing inconsistency rather than clarity; a full-cluster rename is beyond the finding's suggestion and yields marginal benefit. AGENTS.md does not address this naming either way, but the surrounding module's convention is consistent and deliberate. Per the verification criteria, a technically-true observation whose fix makes the code read unlike the rest of the module is not real.

refuted.title: UIImage constant homed on Bundle with a name that overpromises
refuted.reason: The finding's central premise — that the name "appIcon" overpromises because callers "actually get the avatar asset" — is factually wrong. The Avatar.imageset contains only AppIcon-iOS-Default-1024x1024@1x.png, i.e. the literal app icon artwork, so Bundle.appIcon returns exactly what its name says (verified in MuseAmp/Resources/Assets.xcassets/Avatar.imageset/Contents.json). The asset name "Avatar" is the misnomer, and the Bundle.appIcon alias is the corrective layer. Callers are not misled: AlertControllerConfiguration.alertImage (AppDelegate.swift:25) semantically wants the app icon and gets it; the three popup call sites already locally rebind it as placeholderArtwork. The only residue is the mild oddity of homing a UIImage static on Bundle instead of UIImage, but AGENTS.md line 72 explicitly lists Bundle among the Extension-layer system types (Extension+Bundle.swift is a documented file), and "app icon" is conceptually a bundle attribute. The suggested rename to placeholderArtwork would make the alertImage call site worse. Net: cosmetic at most, premise refuted, no real clarity gain from the fix.

refuted.title: Asymmetric inout-threaded header/footer sizing API
refuted.reason: The cited code exists exactly as described at /Users/qaq/Documents/GitHub/MuseAmp/MuseAmp/Extension/Extension+UITableView.swift (lines 11-33): the header variant threads two inout params with a lastWidth short-circuit, the footer variant threads one without it. But the finding's substance collapses on verification. (1) A repo-wide grep (app target, MuseAmpTV symlinks, all packages) finds ZERO call sites for sizeHeaderToFit, sizeFooterToFit, or any headerWidthConstraint/lastHeaderWidth bookkeeping properties — and `git log -S "sizeHeaderToFit"` shows no caller ever existed since the init commit. The claimed harms ("forces every caller to own two pieces of bookkeeping state", "call sites noisy (&headerWidthConstraint, &lastHeaderWidth)", "footers re-run systemLayoutSizeFitting on every call") describe call sites that do not exist; the noisy-call-site quote is fabricated. (2) The asymmetry is plausibly functional, not unexplained drift: the footer variant has an isHidden collapse branch (frame height .leastNonzeroMagnitude) the header lacks, and a footer's required height changes when isHidden toggles even at constant width — so adding the suggested lastWidth short-circuit to the footer could skip a needed relayout after unhiding at the same width, i.e. the proposed "unification" risks introducing a bug rather than improving clarity. (3) The TableAuxiliarySizer wrapper suggestion refactors an API with zero consumers; if anything the actionable issue here is dead code, which is a different finding than the one filed. AGENTS.md says nothing about inout threading or this extension, so conventionEndorsed=false, but the finding fails on its own merits: technically-observable asymmetry with no demonstrated reader or maintainer harm.

refuted.title: Log category string literal "AudioFileImporter" hand-repeated three times in detached task
refuted.reason: The cited code exists exactly as described (literals at lines 435/442/453 inside Task.detached, `self` elsewhere in the file). However, passing a string-literal type name to AppLog inside escaping closures, detached tasks, and static contexts is the dominant, deliberate convention across the entire app target (20+ call sites: DownloadManager+Digger, PlaybackController+Resolution, SongExportPresenter, PlaylistDetailViewController, SettingsViewController+Actions, MainController, AppEnvironment, main.swift). Applying the suggested fix only here would make the file read unlike the rest of the repo. Additionally, the author deliberately captured only `paths` and `apiClient` (lines 417-418) to avoid retaining `self` in the long-running detached task; the suggestion to capture self changes object lifetime for a cosmetic logging concern, and hoisting `AppLog.categoryName(for: self)` is a pattern used nowhere else in the repo. Technically true but not a genuine clarity improvement under repository conventions.

refuted.title: itemCache is a grow-only unbounded dictionary cache
refuted.reason:
The factual observations are accurate: `itemCache` (/Users/qaq/Documents/GitHub/MuseAmp/MuseAmp/Backend/Playback/PlaybackController.swift:29) is written only at PlaybackController+Resolution.swift:100, read at Resolution.swift:64 and via `cachedItem(for:)` (line 437-439), and `QueueState.reset()` clears `trackLookup` but not `itemCache`. Nothing ever evicts it. However, the finding does not hold up as a real clarity/convention defect:

1. The retention is intentional design, not drift. The cache exists precisely as the last-resort fallback branch in `resolvePlayerItem` (Resolution.swift:165-169): when a track's `localFileURL` is nil and the DB path lookup misses, the last-known-good local URL keeps the track playable. `reset()` clearing `trackLookup` while keeping `itemCache` is a visible, deliberate asymmetry in a four-line function, and the behavior is explicitly covered by tests (MuseAmpTests/Playback/PlaybackControllerTests.swift:50, 76, 278 — including persistence across a restored controller). Clearing it on `.libraryDidSync` or queue reset, as suggested, would defeat the tested fallback purpose.

2. The AGENTS.md rule cited ("Use LRUCache for bounded in-memory caches") prescribes which library to use when a bounded cache is wanted; it does not mandate bounding every cache. The repo uses LRUCache where bounds actually matter — heavyweight payloads like artwork palettes (NowPlayingArtworkBackgroundCoordinator, countLimit 32) and preview files (MuseAmpImageView+Preview, countLimit 100). `itemCache` holds tiny `PlayerItem` value structs keyed by local-library track IDs, so worst-case growth is bounded by distinct tracks played per app session — practically negligible memory. The finding itself concedes the staleness concern is masked by the fileExists re-check, so there is no correctness issue either.

3. The suggested fix would degrade the code. `QueueState` is a `nonisolated struct` and `resolvePlayableItems` snapshots the dictionary by value (`let cachedItems = queueState.itemCache`, Resolution.swift:64) for Sendable capture inside `withTaskGroup`. Replacing the dictionary with a reference-type LRUCache breaks that clean value-snapshot pattern, and an eviction limit could silently drop exactly the fallback URLs the cache exists to keep. A technically-true observation whose fix makes the code worse and changes tested behavior is not a real finding.

refuted.title: Document format version literal 1 duplicated
refuted.reason: The cited code exists exactly as described (version = 1 at line 47, decodeIfPresent ?? 1 at line 60 of MuseAmp/Backend/Playlist/PlaylistTransferDocument.swift), but the finding misreads the semantics. Line 47 is the CURRENT format version written on export. Line 60 is the IMPLIED-LEGACY version assigned to documents whose JSON lacks a `version` key — i.e. files written before versioning existed (or hand-crafted ones). That fallback is pinned to 1 forever by definition: when the format is bumped to 2, the memberwise init must change to 2 while the decode fallback must remain `?? 1`, because a version-less file can only be a v1-era file. So the finding's core claim — 'a future bump must currently touch two unrelated lines' — is wrong, and the suggested fix (one shared `static let currentVersion = 1` used in both places) would introduce a latent correctness bug: after a bump, legacy version-less documents would decode as the new current version, defeating the entire purpose of the version field. The repo elsewhere does use named version constants (SyncConstants.protocolVersion, DatabaseFormat.indexSchemaVersion), so a `currentVersion` constant for the export side alone would be stylistically fine, but the finding as stated conflates two distinct concepts and its fix degrades the code. Also note `version` is currently never read by any consumer (PlaylistTransferCoordinator only encodes/decodes), so the duplication has near-zero practical risk today. AGENTS.md says nothing endorsing inline literals here, so conventionEndorsed=false; the finding fails on substance, not convention.

refuted.title: Two conflicting cache-persistence policies for the same LyricsCacheStore
refuted.reason: The factual claims check out (gated write in LyricsService.persistLyricsIfDownloaded vs unconditional write at LyricsReloadService.swift:64, reachable for non-downloaded tracks), but the conflict framing fails. The two writes serve different intents — implicit playback-time loading vs explicit user-initiated reload — and each is self-documenting: the gated helper is named persistLyricsIfDownloaded, and reloadLyrics's doc comment explicitly says "Always persists to the on-disk cache", which is the author deliberately flagging the unconditional behavior — i.e., the finding's own second remedy is already in place. The first remedy (routing reload through the download gate) would change user-visible behavior by breaking persistence for explicit reloads of streamed tracks, so it is not a clarity-neutral fix. No evidence of an actual bug bred by the divergence. At most a one-line cross-reference comment would help, which is cosmetic.

refuted.title: Repeated 64 * 1024 chunk-size literal not hoisted next to maxRequestBufferSize
refuted.reason: The cited literals exist (SyncServer.swift lines 226 and 529), but the finding's premise that they are inconsistent with the file's convention does not hold. maxRequestBufferSize is named because it is used at three sites and in shared testable logic; the two 64 * 1024 literals are each single-use at API call sites whose parameter labels (maximumLength:, ofLength:) already describe them, and 64 * 1024 is the standard self-documenting 64 KiB buffer idiom. The two values are functionally independent, and introducing two identically-valued named constants (or one shared one) would suggest coupling that does not exist. The surrounding Sync module names constants only when reused or semantically opaque, so the current code matches the dominant local convention. The fix is cosmetic at best and arguably reduces clarity.

refuted.title: NetServiceResolver hand-rolls an optional-continuation once-guard instead of the shared helpers
refuted.reason: Code exists as cited, but the finding misapplies the AGENTS.md rule. (1) The rule bans defining ad-hoc box/guard *types* per file; NetServiceResolver defines no type, it uses an optional stored continuation. (2) ContinuationOnceGuard only supports CheckedContinuation<Value, any Error>, so the shared helper literally cannot be used here — the suggestion itself admits new shared infrastructure must be written first. (3) Even with a Never-failure overload, the optional stored continuation cannot be removed: delegate methods are separate functions, so the continuation must be stored across the resolve() call regardless; adding the guard would yield strictly more state (optional property + lock-based guard) and falsely imply a cross-thread race, since NetService delegate callbacks arrive serially on one run loop. The current resume-then-nil idiom is the conventional, clearer pattern for serial delegate-to-continuation bridging, matching how SyncServer differs (closure-scoped continuation with genuinely racy NWListener callbacks).

refuted.title: PreviewCoordinator models one lifecycle with three independent optionals
refuted.reason: The cited code exists as described: PreviewCoordinator (MuseAmp/Interface/Common/MuseAmpImageView/MuseAmpImageView+Preview.swift lines 79-97) holds three optionals and force-unwraps previewItemURL in previewController(_:previewItemAt:). However, the finding fails on three counts. (1) Dominant local convention: the only other QLPreviewControllerDataSource in the repo, ImageQuickLookPreviewPresenter.swift (same Interface/Common area, same author/date), uses the exact same pattern — optional previewItemURL, count = nil ? 0 : 1, and `previewItemURL! as NSURL`. Rewriting PreviewCoordinator as a state enum would make it read unlike its sibling, which the verification rubric says makes a technically-true finding not real. (2) The enum fix would not actually remove the contract reliance: previewItemAt must return a non-optional QLPreviewItem, so in .empty/.loaded states you would still need a fatalError/precondition/dummy-URL fallback — the `!` is just exchanged for a different explicit failure path guarded by the same numberOfPreviewItems contract, which is the standard idiom for this delegate API. (3) The 'implicit combinations' claim is overstated: the coordinator is 19 lines and mutated at exactly two call sites (handleLoadedImageApplied sets all three together; clear() nils all three), and the 'pending' prefix already communicates the awaiting-materialization state. AGENTS.md Property Rules ('avoid unnecessary optionals... if a property can have a meaningful default value') do not condemn these optionals — nil is the meaningful 'nothing loaded yet' value with no sensible default. The crash scenario (clear() racing a presented QLPreviewController) is the same latent narrowness present in the conventionally-accepted sibling and is not a clarity defect of this class specifically.

refuted.title: Assigning onImageLoaded has a hidden replay side effect
refuted.reason: The didSet replay exists as described, but it is a deliberate, load-bearing design: NowPlayingArtworkImageView starts loading artwork in init (Combine subscription) before the controller binds onArtworkLoaded in bindArtworkSectionActions, so the replay-on-assign is required for the popup item image and background coordinator to receive an already-loaded image. The flagged property is the terminal link of a four-file didSet-forwarding chain (NowPlayingCompactPageController → NowPlayingCenterSectionView → NowPlayingAvatarSectionView → MuseAmpImageView), making assignment-time didSet side effects the dominant deliberate convention of this module. AGENTS.md Property Rules explicitly mandate the non-optional closure-property-with-empty-default form for callbacks, and the NowPlayingArtworkShellPresenting protocol requires onArtworkLoaded as a settable property (NowPlayingRelaxedController implements it as a forwarding computed get/set), so the suggested setOnImageLoaded(_:replayingCurrentImage:) method would break the protocol design and read unlike the rest of the repo. Technically-true naming nit, but the fix is a net negative here.

scopeSummaries.summary: This scope is the iOS app's bootstrap chain and adaptive root shell. Entry is manual: main.swift wraps UIApplicationMain in MainActor.assumeIsolated -> AppDelegate (minimal: AlertController theming, scene config, Mac Catalyst Cmd+W/Q interception with a quit-confirmation gate driven by the pure-function MacCatalystTerminationPolicy, background-URLSession completion-handler storage, and a global terminateApplication() -> Never) -> SceneDelegate, which shows BootProgressController as the initial root, builds AppEnvironment off-main (DatabaseManager.initializeSynchronously inside a Task), then cross-fades to MainController and triggers playback restoration plus downloadManager.reconcileOnLaunch(). AppEnvironment is the composition root and sole owner of all backend services (~18 stored lets: APIClient, DatabaseManager, MusicLibraryDatabase, PlaylistStore, DownloadStore, DownloadManager, PlaybackController, lyrics services, AudioFileImporter, etc.), with responsibility-split extensions: +Bootstrap (static DatabaseManager factory injecting RuntimeDependencies closures across the app->MuseAmpDatabaseKit boundary, AppLog sink bridging, Kingfisher cache config), +Events (Combine subscription to databaseManager.events that fans out as amusic.* NotificationCenter posts and refreshes the global TrackTitleSanitizer), and +Transfer (SyncTransferSession factory). MainController is the adaptive root: it computes LayoutMode (.compact/.relaxed/.catalyst) from trait/layout passes and swaps between a child TabBarController (compact: UITab on iOS 18+, legacy viewControllers on 16-17, conditional Search tab keyed off AppPreferences.currentSubsonicConfiguration) and a PopupBarSplitViewController hosting SidebarViewController plus cached per-destination/per-playlist UINavigationControllers. The LNPopupController "Now Playing" bar is managed twice in deliberately duplicated code (TabBarController+Popup and MainController+Popup) binding PlaybackController $snapshot/playbackTimeSubject, with PopupBarPagingHandler translating popup-bar swipes into skipToQueueTrack via LNPopupItem.userInfo queue indexes under a 0.5s cooldown. SceneDelegate also owns all external data entry: file-URL imports (audio extension whitelist, .museampplaylist, .subsonicconfig) coalesced over 200ms and queued until MainController exists, plus museamp://tv URLs carrying base64-JSON SyncReceiverHandshakeInfo with protocol-version gating. Extension/ holds five tiny Extension+ClassName.swift files (Bundle.appIcon, String.sanitizedTrackTitle delegating to TrackTitleSanitizer, UIColor.shiftedHue, UITableView header/footer autosizing, UIView.removeAnimationsRecursively). AppPreferences is a nonisolated static enum facade over ConfigurableKit/UserDefaults holding Subsonic server URL normalization (forces https + /rest suffix), credentials, and sort-option persistence.

Backend/API holds APIClient, the single app-level network orchestration entry point: a final @unchecked Sendable class wrapping SubsonicClientKit's SubsonicMusicService behind intent-level async methods (ping, searchSongs/searchAlbums, album, song, lyrics, playback) plus artwork URL template resolution (resolveMediaURL handles {w}/{h} placeholders, protocol-relative //, scheme-less relative URLs against baseURL). Its mutable configuration (baseURL/username/password) is NSLock-guarded and lazily re-read from AppPreferences.currentSubsonicConfiguration on every synchronizedState() call, hot-rebuilding the underlying service when server settings change; APIClient+Transfer.swift extends it with the LAN sync-transfer HTTP surface (POST /auth bearer-token handshake, GET /manifest with protocol-version gate, GET /track/{id} download with cleanup-on-failure) speaking Backend/Sync JSON models. Backend/Library is the thin bridge between app code and MuseAmpDatabaseKit's DatabaseManager: MusicLibraryDatabase (core + +Tracks/+Downloads/+Ingest/+Bridge extensions) routes reads through synchronous DatabaseManager queries and writes through LibraryCommand (sendSynchronouslyIfSupported first, async send fallback); +Bridge.swift is currently an empty placeholder. AudioFileImporter is the file-picker import pipeline: security-scoped access held for the whole batch, recursive audio discovery, AVAsset metadata inspection, mandatory catalog trackID/albumID extraction from embedded JSON comment (files without it are rejected as noMetadata), three-layer duplicate detection (intra-batch set, existing DB records via case-insensitive title/artist/album + 2s duration tolerance, destination-file existence), staging copy into paths.incomingDirectory, then database.ingestAudioFile to commit, with optional detached-Task background artwork fetch. EmbeddedMetadataReader extracts AVFoundation metadata (title/artist/album/track/disc/genre/composer/releaseDate/lyrics/artwork presence) into AudioTrackRecord values with localized Unknown Artist/Album fallbacks. AudioTrackRecord+AppModels is the adapter seam converting DB records into PlaylistEntry, CatalogSong, PlaybackTrack, SongExportItem, and ImportedTrackMetadata for the Playback/Playlist/Sync/Interface layers. SongLibraryIndexer wraps the .rebuildIndex command; MusicLibraryTrackRemovalService loops .removeTrack synchronously (swallowing errors after logging); TrackArtworkRepairService re-resolves artwork (embedded comment JSON, then song lookup, then album lookup via APIClient at 600x600), caches it atomically, embeds it into the audio file via DownloadArtworkProcessor, and posts .artworkDidUpdate. State ownership: AppEnvironment constructs all these as let properties; persisted state lives in DatabaseManager (WCDB inside MuseAmpDatabaseKit); this scope keeps no caches beyond APIClient's lock-guarded config.

This scope owns the entire download pipeline for remote songs: queueing, URL resolution, transfer via the Digger library, network-aware deferral, post-download finalization (artwork/lyrics/metadata embedding), library ingestion, and crash-safe persistence. The central type is `DownloadManager`, a `@MainActor` class split across five files (core + `+Queue`, `+Digger`, `+Network`, `+Persistence` extensions); it owns all in-memory download state in a `tasks: [String: ActiveDownloadTask]` dictionary keyed by trackID, exposes it to UI via a Combine `CurrentValueSubject` (`tasksPublisher`), and is constructed once in `AppEnvironment` (no singleton). `DownloadStore` is a thin, non-throwing facade over `MusicLibraryDatabase` (MuseAmpDatabaseKit/WCDB) that persists `DownloadJob` records for in-flight work only — completed downloads delete their record and the track lands in the library tracks table; "is downloaded" is derived from the tracks table plus on-disk file existence, never from a stored flag. The task lifecycle is a state machine (`waiting -> resolving -> downloading -> finalizing`, with `waitingForNetwork`, `paused`, `failed` branches), with every state transition mirrored to the database via `persistRecord` so that `reconcileOnLaunch` (called from SceneDelegate) can rehydrate interrupted/queued/finalizing/failed jobs after a relaunch. Finalization moves the Digger cache file to a `.tmp.`-prefixed staging file, concurrently fetches artwork (`DownloadArtworkProcessor`) and lyrics (`DownloadLyricsProcessor`), embeds metadata via `ExportMetadataProcessor` (AVAssetExportSession passthrough re-export including a JSON comment carrying trackID/albumID), then hands the file to `DatabaseManager.send(.ingestAudioFile)` for library ingestion. `NetworkMonitor` (NWPathMonitor wrapper) drives WiFi/cellular/none transitions; cellular downloads are deferred per-track unless explicitly allowed via `allowCellularDownload`. `ExportMetadataProcessor` is also reused outside this scope by `Backend/Lyrics/LyricsReloadService` and `Backend/Sync/SyncPreparedTrackBuilder+Export`, so its embed/verify contract crosses feature boundaries.

MuseAmp/Backend/Playback owns all app-level playback state. The central type is PlaybackController (@MainActor final class, ObservableObject, 553 lines plus four responsibility extensions), which wraps MuseAmpPlayerKit.MusicPlayer, acts as its MusicPlayerDelegate, and is the single source of truth UI observes via the @Published `snapshot` (PlaybackSnapshot) and the high-frequency `playbackTimeSubject`. It is created once in AppEnvironment (iOS) and TVAppContext (tvOS) — every Playback file is mirrored into MuseAmpTV/Backend/Playback via relative symlinks, so this scope compiles in both targets. Internal mutable state is grouped into nested nonisolated structs (QueueState with currentSource/trackLookup/itemCache, RestoreState, SeekState). Supporting value types are PlaybackTrack (the app's canonical track model), PlaybackSnapshot (immutable derived view with computed currentTrack/history/upcoming), PlaybackSource (custom-Codable enum tagging where a queue came from), PlaybackOutputDevice (AVAudioSession route mapping), and PlaybackSessionStore (JSON file persistence of PersistedPlaybackSession/PersistedPlaybackTrack for queue restore across launches). PlaybackController+Resolution.swift is the local-file-only URL resolution layer: it maps PlaybackTracks to PlayerItems by probing track.localFileURL, then database.allTrackRelativePaths() under paths.audioDirectory, then a cached item, and throws PlaybackResolutionError.localFileUnavailable otherwise — there is no remote streaming fallback. Queue PlayerItems get synthetic IDs ("queue-item|UUID|trackID") built/parsed by makeQueueItemID/sourceTrackID so duplicate tracks can coexist in a queue. MuseAmp/Backend/Models is a thin adapter layer: five small extension files that convert between SubsonicClientKit's CatalogSong, MuseAmpDatabaseKit's PlaylistEntry/AudioTrackRecord, PlaybackTrack, and MuseAmpInterfaceKit cell-content structs (SongRowContent, AlbumTrackCellContent, AMNowPlayingQueueTrackPresenting), plus SongExportItem, the value type carrying source URL + sanitized "Artist - Title" base filename for export/share/sync flows (not mirrored to tvOS, which has its own TV* models).

This scope holds two small app-target backend subdomains, both constructed and owned by AppEnvironment (MuseAmp/Application/AppEnvironment.swift) and both delegating actual persistence to MuseAmpDatabaseKit. The Playlist side centers on PlaylistStore, a non-throwing facade over DatabaseManager: every mutation (create/import/delete/rename/cover/addSong/removeSong/move/duplicate/clear/updateLyrics) is expressed as a LibraryCommand sent via databaseManager.sendSynchronously, after which the store re-fetches its in-memory `playlists: [Playlist]` cache and posts `.playlistsDidChange` ("amusic.playlistsDidChange") only when an Equatable diff against the pre-mutation snapshot shows change. It special-cases a sentinel zero-UUID "Liked Songs" playlist (Playlist.swift extension): auto-created at init with a generated heart PNG (LikedSongsPlaylistArtwork), name re-synced to the current locale, metadata backfilled, and auto-deleted when its last song is removed. PlaylistCoverArtworkCache is an actor that renders NxN mosaic covers from entry artwork via Kingfisher, caching in an NSCache plus PNG files under Caches/PlaylistCoverArtworkCache keyed by "v3-{id}-{updatedAt ms}-{songCount}-{sidePixels}", and posting `.playlistArtworkDidUpdate`. PlaylistTransferDocument is the versioned Codable .musiclist export/import format (drops entryID/artworkURL/lyrics; tolerates a legacy `entries` JSON key). The Lyrics side has LyricsService (cache-first read: LyricsCacheStore, then APIClient.lyrics, persisting only for locally downloaded tracks), LyricsReloadService (heavier path: prefer lyrics embedded in the downloaded M4A via AVFoundation, else fetch remote, persist to cache, re-embed into the audio file via ExportMetadataProcessor, post `.lyricsDidUpdate`; also a destructive rebuildAllLyricsIndex that wipes the whole cache then refetches every track sequentially), and LyricsChineseScriptConverter, a pure CFStringTransform Hans/Hant presentation utility with no persistence. UI layers (Interface/Playlist, Interface/NowPlaying, Interface/Sync, Interface/Root) consume these services through AppEnvironment and observe via NotificationCenter and the DatabaseKit events publisher.

This scope holds four backend subdomains of a pure-UIKit iOS/tvOS music app. Backend/Sync implements a complete LAN song-transfer stack: SyncProtocol.swift defines all wire/domain models (version-gated protocol v4 via SyncConstants, SyncEndpoint with IPv6/scope-aware host parsing, SyncConnectionInfo for QR handoff, SyncManifest/SyncManifestEntry, SyncPlaylistSession with expected/unique track-count invariants, SyncTransferError); SyncServer.swift is a hand-rolled HTTP/1.1 server over NWListener (routes POST /auth, GET /manifest, GET /track/{id}, password-to-Bearer-token auth via an NSLock-guarded TokenStore, 1 MiB request cap, 64 KiB chunked file streaming, progress callbacks); SyncTransferSession.swift is the @MainActor orchestrator owning both sender state (password, prepared batch, server, Bonjour advertiser) and receiver state (browser, temp download directory), delegating network client calls to APIClient (APIClient+Transfer.swift) and imports to AudioFileImporter. SyncPreparedTrackBuilder(+Export) prepares per-track temp copies with embedded catalog metadata/lyrics/artwork off the main thread; SyncBonjourAdvertiser/Browser handle NetService publish/discover with role+protocolVersion TXT records; TVPlaylistSessionStore persists and strictly validates a JSON playlist-session manifest on tvOS. Most Sync files are mirrored into MuseAmpTV/Backend/Sync via relative symlinks, so they must compile for both targets (tvOS sender path is stubbed to throw). Backend/Logging/AppLog.swift is the single mandated diagnostics facade wrapping Dog with category-name normalization and a Layout sub-channel. Backend/Supplement holds cross-cutting nonisolated utilities: ConcurrencyHelpers (UncheckedSendableBox, OnceGuard, ContinuationOnceGuard, nonAccumulatingTimer, asyncMapLatest), TrackTitleSanitizer (frequency-based trailing-pattern stripping with a lock-guarded static cache refreshed from library titles), ChineseScriptSearchNormalizer/SearchTextMatcher (Hans/Hant search-form expansion and highlight ranges), AVMetadataHelper, and StringUtilities (sanitizedLogText, isCatalogID). Backend/MenuProviders contains injected UIKit menu factories (Song/Playlist/AddToPlaylist/Playback context menus plus stateless Copy/Download/MenuSection/preview helpers) that fetch data at action time through escaping provider closures and mutate state only through PlaylistStore/PlaybackController. State ownership: SyncTransferSession instances are created per-flow by AppEnvironment+Transfer (iOS) and TVAppContext (tvOS); menu providers hold no domain state of their own.

This scope holds the local-library browse UI for the iOS app target: three list screens (SongLibraryViewController for album groups in Browse/Albums, SongsViewController for flat tracks in Browse/Songs, DownloadsViewController for active download tasks in Browse/Downloads) plus the drill-in AlbumDetailViewController in Browse/AlbumDetail and the shared AlbumNavigationHelper in Browse/Support that constructs/pushes album detail screens, including synthesizing a CatalogAlbum from local AudioTrackRecords when the album exists on disk. All lists use UITableViewDiffableDataSource keyed by lightweight ID enums (LibraryItem/SongsItem/AlbumItem/String trackID) with side dictionaries (albumsByID, tracksByID, searchTracksByID, tasksByTrackID) that cell providers resolve at dequeue time; controllers own only presentation state while all domain state lives behind AppEnvironment services (libraryDatabase/databaseManager, downloadStore/downloadManager, playlistStore, playbackController, lyricsService, musicLibraryTrackRemovalService, audioFileImporter). Interface/Collections is a library of reusable TableBaseCell subclasses (AmSongCell, AmMediaCell/AmMediaRowView, AlbumTrackCell, DownloadProgressCell, PlaylistHeaderCell, skeleton/show-more/search cells) configured exclusively through Hashable value-type *Content structs (SongRowContent, MediaRowContent, AlbumTrackCellContent, AlbumHeaderContent, AMDownloadProgressContent, ArtworkContent), with model-to-content adapter inits living in Backend/Models or in private controller extensions. Interface/Common provides the shared infrastructure: TableBaseCell, MediaDetailViewController base class, EmptyStateView, the Interface animation wrapper + InterfaceStyle/PlatformInterfacePalette style enums, MuseAmpImageView (a Kingfisher-backed image view with manual loadID ticketing, disk-cache decode queue, shimmer, and QuickLook preview), and the Presenters folder of toast/alert/progress/export presenters (SongExportPresenter, PlaybackFeedbackPresenter, DownloadSubmissionFeedbackPresenter, ConfirmationAlertPresenter, ProgressActionPresenter, LyricsReloadPresenter, TrackArtworkRepairPresenter, ImageQuickLookPreviewPresenter). AlbumDetailViewController is the most complex type: it accepts a full album, a stub album, or a bare song/album ID, lazily resolves the real album via APIClient, patches missing artwork from track attributes, and exposes download/playlist/export/playback menus per the album-menu rules. Note for reviewers: the project CLAUDE.md claims TableBaseCell/Interface/EmptyStateView/MediaDetailViewController live in a MuseAmpInterfaceKit package, but no such package exists in this working tree — the files in Interface/Common ARE the canonical implementations here, not shims.

This scope is the full-screen Now Playing experience, presented via LNPopupController (never a tab). Two root controllers exist for the two layout worlds: NowPlayingCompactController (iPhone; hosts NowPlayingCompactPageController, which horizontally pages queue/artwork/lyrics via NowPlayingPagingSectionView) and NowPlayingRelaxedController (iPad/Catalyst; NowPlayingRelaxedShellView with left artwork+transport panel and a right panel toggling lyrics/queue via a segmented control). Both controllers are thin: nearly all behavior is shared through a tower of @MainActor "shell controller" protocols in Support/ (NowPlayingShellController -> NowPlayingPlaybackShellController -> Queue/Artwork/Lyrics/Lifecycle/Transport variants) whose default implementations in `where Self: UIViewController` extensions do the Combine binding, queue action wiring, popup preparation, and suspension handling — effectively protocol mixins. State is owned upstream by Backend's PlaybackController (reached via AppEnvironment); this layer only mirrors it into a locally cached `currentPlaybackSnapshot` and derives presentation through NowPlayingControlIslandViewModel + the pure nonisolated NowPlayingContentMapper (PlaybackSnapshot -> AMNowPlayingContent + BackgroundSource + animation flags). Queue rows are built by AMNowPlayingQueueSnapshotBuilder (history capped at 3, upcoming at 10, occurrence-suffixed IDs for duplicate tracks, footer with remaining count/minutes) and rendered by NowPlayingQueueSectionView, a manual UITableViewDataSource with hand-rolled change detection (NowPlayingQueuePresentationUpdate) and anchored auto-scroll with a 1s programmatic-scroll block window. Lyrics live in LyricTimeline/: LyricTimelineView owns its own Combine pipeline (track-ID changes + .lyricsDidUpdate notification + playbackTimeSubject -> LyricsPhase -> item Snapshot), parsing LRC via LyricParser/LyricTimeline (offset tags, multi-timestamp lines, binary-search active line). LyricSheet/ holds LyricSelectionSheetViewController, a multi-select copy sheet that is the only diffable-data-source table in the scope. Components/ contains the transport view class hierarchy (base NowPlayingTransportView + compact/relaxed subclasses, split into +Actions/+Layout/+Navigation), self-subscribing PlayerControlButton subclasses, a scrubbing ProgressTrackView, and the ColorfulX-backed animated artwork background whose palette is computed by NowPlayingArtworkBackgroundCoordinator (DominantColors on a background queue, LRUCache of 32 keyed by artwork URL string). Notably, Support/NowPlayingLyricsCoordinator.swift appears to be unreferenced in the iOS target (LyricTimelineView+Binding now does its own loading; tvOS has its own TVNowPlayingLyricsCoordinator), making it likely dead code worth a reviewer's eye.

This scope holds four feature-owned UIKit areas of the iOS app target, all programmatic UIKit with no storyboards. Playlist/ contains the playlist library list (PlaylistViewController + Editing/Search/Table extensions, diffable table over PlaylistStore data with sort options, multi-select editing, and local fuzzy search), the playlist detail screen (PlaylistDetailViewController + Actions/Artwork/Menu extensions, with drag-export, reorder/remove via a custom diffable data source subclass, generated/custom cover artwork, and download-all), plus PlaylistTransferCoordinator which imports/exports playlists as JSON documents through document pickers and share sheets. Search/ is a remote-catalog search screen (SearchViewController + Search/Table extensions) backed by a value-type SearchResultsState struct and a UserDefaults-backed SearchHistoryStore, querying APIClient.searchSongs/searchAlbums in parallel with 300 ms Task.sleep debounce, pagination via SearchPage offsets, and result deduplication. Settings/ is a ConfigurableKit-driven StackScrollController (SettingsViewController + API/Actions/Lyrics extensions) covering Subsonic server-profile import/validate/export/delete (ServerProfileImportCoordinator), transfers entry, downloads, lyrics/title tweaks, database and lyrics-index rebuilds, and a self-contained LogViewerController that parses Dog log files. Sync/ is the LAN song-transfer UI: SyncRoleSelectionViewController fans out to a multi-select SyncContentPickerViewController (albums/songs/playlists tabs), sender-side SyncServerStatusViewController and SyncPlaylistAppleTVSenderViewController (Bonjour + QR + temporary password), receiver-side SyncReceiverViewController (discovery, QR scan, manual endpoint+password), and SyncTransferProgressViewController which drives the manifest/compare/download/import phases. State ownership follows the project rule: PlaylistStore (Backend) owns playlist data and controllers re-read it on .playlistsDidChange; SyncTransferSession (Backend/Sync, created per-flow via environment.makeSyncTransferSession()) owns transfer state and feeds local Phase/State enums; search and log-viewer state are controller-local value types. All Sync and Settings screens render by fully tearing down and rebuilding StackScrollController arranged subviews (refreshUI pattern), while Playlist/Search use diffable snapshots with hasAppliedInitialSnapshot gating.

MuseAmpTV is a deliberately thin tvOS receiver shell: its only product feature is receiving a playlist from an iPhone over LAN (Bonjour + QR pairing) and playing it back full-screen with lyrics. Entry is main.swift (MainActor.assumeIsolated + UIApplicationMain, no @main) -> TVAppDelegate -> TVSceneDelegate, which boots through TVBootProgressController (synchronous DatabaseManager init off-main, then restoreSessionStateAtLaunch) before crossfading to TVRootViewController. TVAppContext (split into TVAppContext.swift, +Bootstrap, +Session) is the TV analogue of AppEnvironment and owns all shared dependencies: APIClient, MusicLibraryDatabase, PlaylistStore, DownloadStore, PlaybackController, LyricsService, AudioFileImporter, SyncTransferSession, and TVPlaylistSessionStore, plus the in-memory currentSessionManifest and one-shot pending alerts. Almost the entire Backend/ tree (API, Downloads, Library, Logging, Lyrics, Playback, Playlist, Supplement, Sync) plus Application/AppPreferences.swift are relative symlinks into MuseAmp/ — far broader than the Sync-only mirroring AGENTS.md documents — so the only TV-native backend code is Backend/Models/ (four small AMTV-prefixed flow/content types) and Backend/Sync/TVSessionStateAdapter.swift. TVSessionStateAdapter (714 lines) is the central state machine: a private TransferPhase enum (waiting/connecting/receiving/importing/completed/disconnected/failed) is combined with the playback snapshot and session manifest into a computed AMTVLibrarySessionState, which TVRootViewController collapses to AMTVRootFlowState and renders by toggling isHidden on three pre-allocated surfaces (TVQRPairingView, TVTransferProgressView, TVNowPlayingController) with snapshot crossfades and tvOS focus updates. TVNowPlayingController hosts a split artwork/transport + read-only lyric timeline view, observing $snapshot and playbackTimeSubject, with a local TVNowPlayingLyricsCoordinator and a TV-specific LRC parser/timeline (TVLyricParser, TVLyricTimeline) feeding TVLyricTimelineView. State ownership: transfer phase lives in TVSessionStateAdapter, session manifest + alerts in TVAppContext, playback in the shared PlaybackController; views are stateless renderers driven by a single reloadContent() pass.

MuseAmpDatabaseKit is a Swift 6 SwiftPM package whose only dependency is WCDBSwift (Lakr233/wcdb-spm-prebuilt); it owns the file-backed local music library: two SQLite databases, the on-disk audio tree, artwork/lyrics caches, and audit reporting. The central type is `DatabaseManager` (an `@unchecked Sendable` final class) which composes stateless internal structs — `IndexStore` (library_index.sqlite: the derived, rebuildable track index), `StateStore` (library_state.sqlite: authoritative user state — download_jobs, playlists, playlist_entries, meta tables), `LibraryScanner` (disk-to-index rebuild), `LibraryFileManager` (staged .tmp moves into `Audio/<albumID>/<trackID>.<ext>`), `CacheCoordinator` (artwork .jpg / lyrics .lrc caches plus orphan pruning), `DownloadCoordinator` (download-job rows only; actual transport lives in the app target), and `DatabaseBootstrapper` (opens both stores, stamps schema versions in index_meta/state_meta). The public surface is command/event shaped: callers `send(_: LibraryCommand)` (sync fast paths via `sendSynchronouslyIfSupported`, async paths `rebuildIndex`/`ingestAudioFile` isolated to the `@DatabaseActor` global actor) and observe a Combine `events: AnyPublisher<LibraryEvent, Never>` for change notifications (tracksChanged, downloadsChanged, playlistsChanged, cache-changed, audit). `initialize()` must run exactly once (NSLock + precondition); stores are optionals and every public query calls `requireInitialized()` then degrades to empty results if a store is nil. WCDB row types under `Internal/WCDB/` never escape the package — they map to public immutable value models under `Models/` via `init(from:)`/`toModel()`, honoring the repo rule that app code only sees model types. `Storage/LibraryPaths` owns the whole directory layout (Documents/OfflineLibrary base excluded from backup, Caches/OfflineLibrary/LibraryArtwork for purgeable artwork) plus one-time migrations from Application Support and legacy cache directory names. `RuntimeDependencies` injects host-app closures (inspectAudioFile, resolveDownloadURL, fetchLyrics, fetchArtworkData, requestHeaders, setScreenAwake) so the package performs no networking itself; `DatabaseManager+Audit` produces an `AuditSnapshot` of counts, orphans, invalid paths, temp files, and schema mismatches.

SubsonicClientKit is a self-contained, dependency-free SwiftPM package (swift-tools 6.2, iOS 15/macOS 12) that implements the Subsonic REST API (v1.16.1, client name "museamp") behind one central type: `SubsonicMusicService`, a Sendable final class offering async `ping()`, `search()`, `album(id:)`, `song(id:)`, `playback(id:)`, and `lyrics(id:)`. Every call funnels through a single generic `perform(_:cache:decode:)` pipeline that builds URLs via the `SubsonicEndpoint` enum (which owns action names, query items, and per-endpoint `cacheIdentifier` strings), unwraps the `subsonic-response` JSON envelope, and maps raw `Subsonic*` DTOs (Models/Subsonic/, with lossy string/int decoding for inconsistent servers) into Apple-Music-shaped `Catalog*` public models (Models/, with attributes/relationships split per file). State ownership: the service owns five per-type in-memory `ResponseCache` actors (TTL hardcoded to infinity, 7-day stale window for failure fallback), a `RequestCoalescer` actor that dedupes concurrent identical requests, an NSLock-guarded `authMode` that auto-falls back from MD5-token auth to plain-password auth on auth-shaped failures, and an optional injected `CacheStorageProvider` for versioned raw-Data disk caching (currently nil in the app — only package tests exercise it). Authentication credentials are embedded as query parameters in every URL the package emits, including artwork template URL strings stored inside Catalog models and `PlaybackInfo.playbackURL`, which then cross into the app's Backend layers. Song-type searches optionally fan out per-song getSong enrichment via a task group, silently falling back to the thinner row on error. The only app-side consumer is `MuseAmp/Backend/API/APIClient.swift` (plus tvOS bootstrap), which constructs and rebuilds the service on configuration change; the package itself does no logging and no UIKit, communicating failures purely through the localized `APIError` enum.

MuseAmpPlayerKit is a self-contained Swift 6 (strict concurrency, swiftLanguageModes [.v6]) SwiftPM package providing queue-based audio playback with gapless preloading, lock-screen/Control Center integration, and a delegate-based API; its only dependency is Kingfisher (artwork). The central type is `MusicPlayer`, a `@MainActor final class` in Player/MusicPlayer.swift, split across responsibility extensions (+Playback transport and loadAndPlay/continueWithCurrentEngineItem, +Queue public queue editing, +Observation KVO/notification handling and item-failure advance, +Restoration restorePlayback, +Logging describe helpers). Queue truth lives in `PlaybackQueue` (Queue/PlaybackQueue.swift), a value-type struct owning the canonical `items` array, `currentIndex` (an index into the derived `effectiveOrder`, not into `items`), `playedIndices` history, and a `shufflePermutation`; consumers only ever see immutable `QueueSnapshot` values. The AVFoundation layer is abstracted behind the internal `AudioPlaybackEngine` protocol, implemented by `AVPlayerEngine` wrapping an `AVQueuePlayer` plus a tracked `preloadedItem` for gapless transitions (tests inject MockAudioPlaybackEngine through the internal init). MediaCenter/ holds three collaborators owned by MusicPlayer: `MediaCenterCoordinator` (selects `SessionMediaCenterBackend` using MPNowPlayingSession on iOS 16+/tvOS 14+ versus `LegacyMediaCenterBackend` using MPNowPlayingInfoCenter), `NowPlayingInfoManager` (mutates a `currentInfo` dictionary mirror and republishes it wholesale, including a 10-second auto-expiring lyric subtitle and async Kingfisher artwork via `ArtworkLoader`), and `RemoteCommandManager` (wires MPRemoteCommandCenter targets to a weak player reference). `AudioSessionManager` configures AVAudioSession (long-form audio policy with fallback) and routes interruption/route-change notifications back into pause/play. Models/ contains the small public Sendable value types (`PlayerItem`, `PlaybackState`, `RepeatMode`, `TransitionReason`, `QueueSnapshot`), and Logging/ defines the injected `MusicPlayerLogger` protocol with a `NoopMusicPlayerLogger` default. Nothing is persisted inside the package; session persistence and URL resolution live in the app's `MuseAmp/Backend/Playback/` (PlaybackController, PlaybackSessionStore), which is the package's primary consumer along with the MuseAmpTV shell.
